turns.user_message: I smoke bidis. One packet a day, about 15 to 16. I've been doing this since I was around 18 or 20.
step2_rag_chunks: Bidi smoke is more concentrated than cigarette smoke and delivers higher levels of nicotine despite being thinner. This means bidi dependence can be as strong as or stronger than cigarette dependence, and withdrawal symptoms can be equally intense. Standard cessation support â€” behavioral strategies and nicotine replacement â€” is just as applicable and effective for bidi smokers.
The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.
Reward yourself for milestones such as staying smoke-free for a day, a week, or a month.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

This is not about having someone watch over you â€” it is about having someone to call when the urge is strong, or someone who will not offer you a cigarette. step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? That conflict is not weakness, it is just how change works. It helps to talk about both sides openly rather than pushing the doubts aside.
If you slip and smoke a cigarette, it does not mean you have failed. Many people attempt quitting several times before succeeding.

step2_rag_chunks: If you have tried to quit before, that experience is actually useful information â€” not a failure. What worked even a little? What made it harder? Most people who successfully quit have tried several times before it sticks. Each attempt teaches you something about your own pattern.

That specific information is what makes the next attempt more informed. Most people who successfully quit smoked again at some point during the process.
Withdrawal symptoms usually peak within the first few days after quitting and gradually decrease over time.

Given your relationship with your wife, I'd like to ask: Would you be willing to have an open and honest conversation with her about your feelings and needs regarding quitting smoking, and see if she's open to learning more about how she can support you in a way that feels authentic to her?
Patient: I don't know... I've been doing this for so long, and every time I try to quit, it feels like I'm failing. I'm not sure if she'll think I'm weak or something if I ask for her help.

Therapist: You feel like you've failed every time you try to quit, and that's making it hard to ask for your wife's help. What is one thing you've done in the past that might have worked a little bit, or made quitting feel slightly more manageable, even if it was just for a short time?

Patient: I think I was able to quit for a few days once, when I was working on a project away from my regular workplace. There, nobody smoked, so I didn't have that trigger. I managed for about three or four days, but then I went back to work and it was hard to resist.

Therapist: Since you were able to quit for a few days when you were away from your regular workplace, it sounds like avoiding certain situations or environments might be helpful in quitting. What do you think about the idea of creating a list of places or situations that trigger your urge to smoke, so we can start working on strategies to avoid or cope with them?
Patient: That might help, I guess. But what about when I'm not at those places? What am I supposed to do then?

That might help, I guess. But what about when I'm not at those places? What am I supposed to do then?

step2_rag_chunks: The 4D technique helps manage cravings: Delay smoking for a few minutes, take Deep breaths slowly, Drink water, and Do something else to distract yourself.
Exercise can help improve mood and reduce nicotine cravings.

When a craving hits, delaying the response by even 5 to 10 minutes is often enough for it to pass. Tell yourself you will smoke in 10 minutes if you still want to â€” and then do something else for those 10 minutes. Most of the time the urge has weakened significantly by the time the 10 minutes are up.

It is completely normal to want to quit and also feel unsure about it at the same time. Most people feel this way â€” one part of them wants to stop, another part is not ready.
